--- Page 1 ---
Aceso: Carbon-Aware and Cost-Effective Microservice Placement
for Small and Medium-sized Enterprises
Georgia Christofidi
IMDEA Software Institute
Universidad Politécnica de Madrid
georgia.christofidi@imdea.org
Francisco Álvarez-Terribas
Telefonica Research
francisco.alvarezterribas@telefonica.com
Ioannis Roumpos
IMDEA Software Institute
Universidad Politécnica de Madrid
giannis.roumpos@imdea.org
Nicolas Kourtellis
Keysight Technologies
nkourtellis@gmail.com
Jesus Omaña Iglesias
Telefonica Research
jesusalberto.omana@telefonica.com
Thaleia Dimitra Doudali
IMDEA Software Institute
thaleia.doudali@imdea.org
ABSTRACT
Microservices are a dominant architecture in cloud computing,
offering scalability and modularity, but also posing complex de-
ployment challenges. As data centers contribute significantly to
global carbon emissions, carbon-aware scheduling has emerged
as a promising mitigation strategy. However, most existing solu-
tions target batch, high-performance, or serverless workloads and
assume access to global-scale infrastructure. Such an assumption
does not hold for many national or regional small to medium-sized
enterprises (SMEs) with microservice applications, which represent
the real-world majority. In this paper, we present Aceso, an Adap-
tive Carbon- and Efficiency-aware placement for microservices that
considers carbon, cost, and latency constraints. Aceso dynamically
places microservices across geographically constrained regions us-
ing a scalable optimization strategy that leverages insight-based
search space pruning techniques. Evaluation on a real-world de-
ployment shows that Aceso quickly adapts to real-time changes in
workload and carbon intensity and reduces carbon emissions by
37.4% and operational cost by 3.6%, on average, compared to a static
deployment within a single country, while consistently meeting
SLOs. In this way, Aceso enables carbon- and cost-aware microser-
vice deployment for latency-sensitive applications in regionally
limited infrastructures for SMEs.
1
INTRODUCTION
The explosive growth of generative AI is reshaping digital infras-
tructure, pushing cloud platforms to unprecedented levels of scale
and complexity [99, 116]. Behind the scenes, microservice archi-
tectures provide the modular and resilient backbone that enables
AI systems to train, deploy, and serve billions of requests effi-
ciently [76, 78, 81, 90, 92, 96, 110]. While this combination acceler-
ates innovation, it also drives a dramatic rise in energy consumption
and pushes carbon footprint to unprecedented levels [103, 107, 116].
Over the past years, data centers worldwide contributed signifi-
cantly to carbon emissions, and their impact is expected to increase
further [37, 89, 104]. This trend underscores the urgent need to
optimize microservice deployment not only for performance and
cost but also to minimize the carbon footprint, ensuring a more
sustainable digital infrastructure [10, 98].
Carbon-aware computing aims to reduce the environmental im-
pact of cloud workloads by aligning their execution with periods or
locations of lower carbon intensity. This can be achieved through
temporal shifting, by delaying execution to times when clean en-
ergy is more available [58, 62, 115], or spatial shifting, by moving
workloads to regions with greener electricity [56, 117]. Prior work
has explored these strategies for edge [117], serverless [56, 62],
distributed ML training [77], batch and high-performance work-
loads [57, 58]. However, microservices remain underexplored due
to their latency sensitivity, tight inter-service communication, and
dynamic behavior. Their performance often depends on low-latency
interactions between components [48, 52, 130], making it difficult to
distribute them across distant regions. Moreover, their modular and
evolving nature requires placement strategies, both fine-grained
and responsive to workload changes [29, 113]. While systems like
Oakestra [19], Astraea [125], Nautilus [48], and Imbres [71] offer ad-
vanced orchestration for microservices, they focus on performance
and resource efficiency and not on their carbon impact.
Many existing carbon-aware solutions also assume access to
global-scale cloud infrastructure [14, 57, 58, 101, 103], where work-
loads can be flexibly shifted across regions or time zones. This as-
sumption holds for serverless platforms like AWS Lambda [56, 62],
which abstract away infrastructure and enable global deployment
with minimal effort. In contrast, microservices are typically de-
ployed on managed Kubernetes clusters or private clouds with
limited regional reach. In addition, national companies operating
within a single country or the EU may be constrained by data
sovereignty and privacy regulations, restricting deployment out-
side European regions [50, 56, 65]. Moreover, services such as e-
banking [37] have geographically concentrated user traffic [66],
making global-scale strategies impractical and cost-inefficient. These
constraints are particularly pronounced for small and medium-sized
enterprises (SMEs), which account for 99% of businesses in the EU
and employ over 60% of the workforce [4], underscoring their role
as the real-world majority. Despite their dominant economic role,
SMEs often lack access to hyperscale, globally distributed infras-
tructure, yet collectively contribute significantly to overall energy
consumption [3]. Therefore, SMEs are critical stakeholders in the
transition toward sustainable cloud computing.
At the same time, organizations of all sizes face mounting pres-
sure to reduce their carbon footprint. Regulatory frameworks such
as the European Green Deal [41] and the European Climate Law [43],
along with sustainability reporting standards, are driving the adop-
tion of greener practices. Financial instruments, including carbon
taxes [73], emission trading schemes [42], and green procurement
1
arXiv:2603.10768v1  [cs.DC]  11 Mar 2026


--- Page 2 ---
incentives [40], are turning carbon efficiency into a business im-
perative aligned with global sustainability goals, such as the UN
Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) [109]. For SMEs with lim-
ited infrastructure flexibility, this is both a challenge (operating
under strict latency, regulatory, and resource constraints) and an
opportunity to adopt carbon-aware, cost-efficient, regionally de-
ployable solutions that support global climate goals. Therefore, this
paper addresses the following question:
How can SMEs deploy microservice applications in a carbon-efficient
and cost-effective manner within geographically constrained infras-
tructures, while maintaining application performance?
To answer this question, we present Aceso1: an Adaptive Carbon-
and Efficiency-aware microservice placement system that jointly
optimizes carbon emissions and monetary cost, while respecting
latency constraints. Aceso employs a scalable genetic algorithm
that rapidly identifies near-optimal microservice placements across
available regions that have heterogeneous carbon intensity. Given
the potentially exponential size of the search space, Aceso leverages
a key insight to effectively reduce it: it prioritizes the offloading of
microservice subtrees that (i) activate early in the request execu-
tion and (ii) do not belong to the critical path, which is the longest
dependency chain amongst microservices determining end-to-end
latency. A microservice subtree denotes a branch of the invocation
call graph comprising services triggered together during request
processing. By focusing on early, non-critical microservice sub-
trees, Aceso eliminates infeasible or low-impact decisions, reducing
optimization overhead while maintaining latency guarantees. By
placing selected microservices in greener regions, Aceso achieves
up to 37.4% carbon emissions reduction and up to 3.6% operational
cost savings, on average, compared to a static, single-region deploy-
ments, while maintaining service-level objectives (SLOs). Overall,
Aceso enables both carbon- and cost-aware microservice placement
for latency-sensitive applications in regionally constrained infras-
tructures. The specific paper contributions are:
• A characterization of carbon reduction opportunities for mi-
croservices within geographically constrained infrastructures,
revealing the insight that selectively offloading early-activated,
non-critical subtrees to greener regions can reduce emissions
without compromising performance (Section 3).
• The design of Aceso, including a dynamic optimization strategy
that adapts to workload patterns and carbon intensity using a
scalable genetic algorithm (Section 4).
• A comprehensive evaluation of Aceso, demonstrating significant
reductions in carbon footprint and cost while maintaining appli-
cation performance in a scalable and stable manner (Section 5).
• To facilitate community adoption and engagement, we will open
source the software of Aceso, alongside a high-fidelity simula-
tor of large-scale geo-distributed microservice placement with
plugable optimization strategies.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents
relevant background knowledge and Section 3 reveals motivational
observations. Then, Section 4 describes in detail Aceso, while Sec-
tion 5 presents its thorough experimental evaluation. Section 6
1In ancient Greek mythology, Aceso (pronounced Akesó) was the Greek goddess of
well-being. Unlike her sister Panacea (Cure-All), she represented the process of curing
rather than the cure itself.

Figure 1: Hourly carbon intensity across European and North
American cloud regions across one week of August.
compares Aceso against related works and, finally, Section 7 dis-
cusses potential limitations and future work.
2
BACKGROUND
2.1
Microservice Applications
Microservices represent a widely adopted architectural style in
which applications are decomposed into small, self-contained ser-
vices. Each service is responsible for a specific function, such as user
authentication, data storage, or recommendation logic, and can be
developed, deployed, and scaled independently. This modularity en-
ables faster development cycles, fault isolation, and flexible scaling.
In real-world deployments, microservices typically form a directed
acyclic graph (DAG), where nodes represent services and edges
capture inter-service communication and dependencies. Figure 3a
illustrates such a DAG for a social network application from Death-
StarBench [51], where users compose posts and read their own or
others’ timelines. It consists of 24 microservices (M0-M11 plus 2
microservices per database) some of which are responsible for com-
putation and others for reading or writing from databases. As shown
in the figure, microservices are activated in a temporal sequence
during request processing, marked with increasing color intensity.
Early-activated microservices, such as the Unique ID and Media
Services, are triggered in parallel at the beginning of a request to
extract essential information, like generating a post identifier or
uploading media. In contrast, late-activated microservices, such
as Post Storage or Home Timeline rendering, are invoked closer to
the end of the pipeline to persist and display the final content.
This creates a temporal dependency across the microservices, a
critical path, that is the longest chain of dependent services that
must be traversed to complete a request [52, 128, 130]. Many real-
world applications, such as online banking, messaging platforms,
e-commerce checkouts, and video streaming, are latency-sensitive
and must meet strict Service Level Objectives (SLOs) [25, 51, 131],
such as P95 or P99 latency under 300 ms [28]. These requirements
limit the flexibility to distribute services geographically, especially
those on the critical path, and highlight the need for system-level
solutions that preserve performance through efficient microservice
placement and resource management [19, 48, 125].
Microservice Deployment Scale in SMEs. While hyperscalers
like Uber [15, 93] and Netflix [93, 105, 108] operate thousands of
microservices (e.g., Uber reports nearly 4,000 [131]), these figures do
2

UAE (Dubai)

Mumbai
Kuala Lumpur
Thailand

Sydney
Osaka
Tokyo
Auckland
Hong Kong
0
50
Monthly Cost
(USD)
Figure 2: Monthly cloud cost across global regions, colored
by continent.
not represent typical deployments. Surveys show that almost half of
organizations run fewer than 100 services, and only about 3% exceed
1,000 [34]. Small businesses often stay below 10 services, whereas
medium-sized enterprises typically manage 10–200 [111]. Crossing
50 services is widely seen as a sign of growing complexity [111].
Given that SMEs account for 99% of EU companies [4], solutions
targeting applications with tens to a hundred microservices are
most relevant to the real-world majority.
2.2
Carbon Intensity
The environmental cost of computing is largely determined by
the carbon intensity of electricity in the region where workloads
execute [56, 69, 101]. Carbon intensity refers to the amount of
carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2eq) emitted per kilowatt-hour (kWh)
of electricity produced. It varies significantly across geographical
locations and time, depending on the local energy mix, i.e., the
proportion of renewable versus fossil-fuel-based generation [2].
Figure 1 shows hourly carbon intensity (CI) for major cloud regions
in Europe and North America over one week in August 2023 using
public data available from Electricity Maps [2]. At that time, we
were able to download the data for free; however, more recent data
is no longer accessible with our account.
We observe that, across both continents, clear day–night patterns
appear: CI tends to drop around midday and rise in the evening
due to demand cycles and renewable variability. For example, solar
energy peaks at noon and declines after sunset, increasing reliance
on fossil generation [2]. Regions with stable baseload power (e.g.,
nuclear, hydro) show minimal variation, while those with high
renewable shares fluctuate more [2]. Due to these reasons, Euro-
pean regions exhibit lower and more diverse carbon intensity (CI)
fluctuations compared to North America.
Figure 1 shows that some regions are consistently greener, such
as Paris and Stockholm in Europe and Montreal in North America.
However, this does not imply that a “follow-the-sun” strategy is op-
timal, as it assumes a global footprint capable of shifting workloads
across far-apart regions [56, 58, 62, 115, 117]. In practice, many
organizations cannot move workloads across continents due to reg-
ulatory or infrastructure constraints. When geography is limited,
follow-the-sun becomes infeasible, and carbon-aware scheduling
must operate within a smaller regional footprint, making local
temporal variations far more relevant.
2.3
Financial Feasibility
Figure 2 illustrates the monthly cost of an AWS t3.medium in-
stance [8] across global regions as a representative pricing trend.
The narrow cost variation creates an important insight: greener
regions are not necessarily more expensive than carbon-heavy ones.
For example, Stockholm, which is one of the lowest-carbon regions,
offers the lowest cost among EU options, making expansion to
greener regions financially viable. However, as noted in Section 2.2,
“follow-the-sun” strategies are not always viable. Instead, identify-
ing a set of regions that enables carbon- and cost-efficient operation
is crucial, particularly for SMEs, where even small price differences
compound over many instances and long deployments. Expanding
to greener regions without significant cost penalties allows SMEs
to meet sustainability goals while maintaining financial efficiency.
3
INSIGHTS FROM A REAL DEPLOYMENT
To investigate the feasibility of carbon-aware microservice deploy-
ment within geographically constrained infrastructures, we con-
duct a real-world experimental study. The goal of this study is to
extract insights that will later inform the design of our adaptive
microservice placement solution, capable of dynamically optimiz-
ing for carbon and cost efficiency while preserving service-level
objectives (SLOs).
Experimental Setup. We consider a European small to medium-
sized enterprize (SME) headquartered in Spain, operating a latency-
sensitive microservice application. Motivated by sustainability goals
and cost constraints, the company explores expanding its cloud
deployment to greener regions. Sweden emerges as a promising
candidate due to its consistently low carbon intensity and competi-
tive pricing. We focus on Spain and Sweden as illustrative regions:
Spain represents a large, moderately carbon-intensive country with
locally constrained deployment options, while Sweden offers a
greener and more cost-effective alternative, as shown in Figures 1
and 2. This contrast allows us to isolate the impact of spatial of-
floading within a realistic EU context.
To assess regional placement trade-offs, we deploy the Death-
StarBench social network application [1, 51] on AWS t3 instances
in Spain and Sweden, reflecting SME-scale deployments and cost
trends (Figure 2). We evaluated static configurations, namely:
• Single MS: Move only one microservice at a time from Spain
to Sweden. This configuration isolates the impact of moving the
unique ID service (M5), media service (M6), user service (M4)
and post storage service (M11).
• Subtree: Move a connected group of microservices that form
a subtree in the DAG. This configuration preserves internal de-
pendencies across the microservices of the subtree. For example,
moving M2, M3, M7 moves the full subtree of the Text Service,
as shown in Figure 3a.
• Layer: Move all microservices that are at the same layer, mean-
ing at a certain distance from the databases. This configuration
targets performance optimization by relocating entire horizontal
tiers of the DAG. For example, moving M4, M6-M7, M9–M11
moves all MS with distance 1, that are adjacent to the DBs.
Table 3b summarizes these placements. Offloading is limited by
latency and data sovereignty, so frontend services and databases
remain always in Spain for responsiveness.
3

placement configurations in the real-world experiment.
Analysis of Results. Figure 4 compares placement configurations
across four dimensions: carbon emissions, monthly cost, end-to-
end latency, and SLO violations (Table 3b). In all the subfigures, the
lower the bars the better.
Figure 4a shows the relative reduction in carbon emissions, com-
puted as the cumulative sum of the energy consumed by each
microservice multiplied by the carbon intensity of the region where
it executes. Moving single microservices such as M5, M6, M4, M11
yields negligible benefits (3%–5% reduction), whereas offloading
larger subtrees like M2-M7 and M3-M7, M9-M11 achieves sub-
stantial savings (21% and 28%, respectively). Moving entire layers
follows a more predictable trend, with emissions decreasing steadily
as additional layers are moved to Sweden. Moving all internal mi-
croservices (except frontend and DBs) delivers the highest reduction
(43%), at the cost of significant latency penalties discussed later.
Figure 4b shows the normalized monthly cost for operating cloud
resources across Spain and Sweden, including the overhead of main-
taining container images pre-replicated in both regions for fast

(d) SLO violations.
Figure 4: Comparison of carbon, monthly cost, latency and
SLO violations for the placement configurations of Table 3b.
migration. Trends mirror carbon emissions reduction since both
depend on the number of services moved to Sweden. Sweden’s lower
pricing (Figure 2) makes greener deployments attractive, with dif-
ferences up to 3%. Although the 1.8% reduction ($21/month) of
moving the M2-M7 subtree may seem modest for our benchmark,
it can translate to significant savings for larger deployments, as we
will show in Section 5.
Figure 4c shows that average end-to-end latency varies widely
across placement configurations. While some configurations re-
main close to the baseline (63ms), others incur severe penalties. For
example, moving the M2-M3,M7-M11 subtrees incurs latency of
334ms, comparable to relocating all internal services (except the
frontend and databases) to Sweden. These differences cannot be ex-
plained by the number of microservices moved: offloading a single
service (M5, M6, M4, M11) yields latencies ranging in 68ms-153ms,
and configurations such as M2-M7 and M4,M6-M7,M9-M11, which
each moves six services, yield latencies ranging 111ms-287ms. This
highlights that latency is not determined by how many services are
relocated, rather by which services and their activation order in the
dependency call graph, underscoring the complexity of placement
decisions.
Activation order. In the social network application of DeathStar-
Bench [1, 51], microservices are activated in the following order:
𝑀0 →𝑀1 →(𝑀5 ∥𝑀6 ∥𝑀4) →(𝑀2 →𝑀3 ∥𝑀7) →(𝑀10 ∥
𝑀11) →𝑀8 →𝑀9. Arrows (→) indicate sequential activation,
while double vertical bars (∥) denote microservices that execute
4


--- Page 5 ---
in parallel within the same stage. Figure 3a also shows the activa-
tion order, where darker shades of the microservice boxes indicate
later activation. A request first reaches the Frontend (M0), which
triggers the Compose Post Service (M1). Then the User (M4), the
Media (M6) and the Unique ID Service (M5) run in parallel to fetch
user metadata, process media and generate a post ID. Next, the
Text Service subtree (M2, M3, M7) handles text analysis, including
URL-shortening and uploading user mentions. Finally, the write-
oriented services, like Home Timeline (M8, M9), User Timeline
(M10), and Post Storage (M11), update timelines and persist the
post. This sequence ensures all computations and all reads finish
before writing to databases.
In Figure 4c we observe that placements which move late-activated
nodes, such as M8-M11, introduces cross-region dependencies at
the tail of execution, amplifying end-to-end latency. For example,
when moving the M2-M3, M7-M11 subtree, average latency exceeds
334ms. In contrast, placements that offload only early-activated
subtrees, such as M2-M7, perform significantly better because any
cross-region communication occurs early in the pipeline, reducing
its impact on overall latency. Figure 4d shows the % of requests
that violated their SLO. The SLO is set to 300 ms for end-to-end
latency, which is a typical value for the social network application
of DeathStarBench [28, 71]. We observe that placements that move
late-activated nodes exhibit up to 39% violations, whereas the rest
of the placements maintain near-zero violations.
Among all static configuration, moving the M2 - M7 subtree
achieves the best overall trade-off, reducing carbon emissions to
0.79× the Spain baseline (21% reduction), lowering monthly cost to
0.982× (about 1.8% cheaper), and maintaining latency at 111.13 ms
with only 1.67% SLO violations. Section 5 confirms this observa-
tion by showing that this placement is also selected as the optimal
placement of our proposed system Aceso.
Key Insights. The analysis from a real deployment reveals that
carbon and cost reduction scale predictably with microservice of-
floading to greener regions, but latency does not. While moving
more microservices to greener regions consistently lowers carbon
emissions and monetary cost, latency depends on the specific place-
ment of services across regions. Relocating late-activated microser-
vices or disrupting the critical path introduces severe penalties. In
contrast, offloading subtrees that are activated early yields much
better results, as cross-region communication happens early in
the process, minimizing its overall impact. This complex interac-
tion between activation order, dependency graph structure, and
regional factors makes placement decisions inherently challenging,
motivating the design of Aceso in Section 4.
4
ACESO SYSTEM DESIGN
4.1
Overview and Objectives
We introduce Aceso, a system that enables adaptive, carbon- and
cost-aware microservice placement across geo-distributed regions
for latency-sensitive applications. Unlike prior work that assumes
access to global-scale infrastructure, such as that available to hyper-
scalers like Google or AWS, Aceso is designed for the real-world
majority: national or local companies and small to medium-sized
enterprises (SMEs) operating within constrained geographic en-
vironments and policies. These organizations often face limited
Incoming 
User Traffic
Traffic Forecaster
Workload Profiler
Placement Optimizer
Scheduler
Predicted Traffic
MS 
Size
Best Placement
Per MS (Energy, 
Latency, Size)
Input from 
Application Owner 
& External APIs
Microservices
Figure 5: System Components of Aceso.
resource availability, strict data sovereignty regulations, and het-
erogeneous resource capacities, making traditional carbon-aware
strategies impractical. Aceso addresses these constraints directly,
enabling sustainable microservice deployment without requiring
global reach.
At its core, Aceso solves a constrained multi-objective optimiza-
tion problem to determine where microservices should be placed
across available regions. Aceso balances three key objectives:
[O1] Carbon Efficiency: Minimize emissions by leveraging re-
gions with cleaner energy sources.
[O2] Cost Efficiency: Lower the cost of expanding operations to
greener regions.
[O3] Latency Compliance: Ensure service-level objectives (SLOs)
are consistently met.
Aceso uses forecasted traffic patterns and real-time carbon in-
tensity data to guide placement decisions. By default, it jointly
optimizes for carbon and cost, ensuring low emissions and SLO
compliance. Application owners can adjust optimization weights to
prioritize carbon reduction, cost savings, or both, allowing Aceso to
adapt to diverse operational goals. Figure 5 illustrates the system
architecture, which consists of four components:
1. Traffic Forecaster: Uses a machine learning model to predicts
short-term traffic volumes from historical patterns (Section 4.2).
2. Workload Profiler: Maps forecasted traffic to per-microservice
metrics, such as latency, energy, and resource use, via hash-table
lookups populated during offline profiling (Section 4.3).
3. Placement Optimizer: Solves a single-objective placement opti-
mization problem using a scalable genetic algorithm, with search
space pruning via region filtering and microservice pinning. It
uses real-time carbon, cost, and inter-region latency data from
external APIs (Section 4.4).
4. Scheduler: Enacts placement decisions by provisioning resources,
migrating services, and rerouting traffic while ensuring SLO com-
pliance (Section 4.5).
Together, these components allow Aceso to operate effectively in
dynamic, geo-distributed cloud environments, enabling sustainable
and cost-effective microservice deployments without compromising
performance.
Input Parameters. The following inputs are provided by the ap-
plication owner to Aceso:
• Application. Access to the full microservice application, to
manage placement and analyze inter-service dependencies and
critical paths.
• Latency SLOs. End-to-end application latency constraints, so
that Aceso can operate under SLO compliance.
5


--- Page 6 ---
• Candidate regions. A list of allowed deployment regions, con-
strained by regulatory or business requirements (e.g., GDPR).
• Optimization weights. Owners may specify the 𝑤carbon and
𝑤cost weights to prioritize carbon reduction, cost savings, or both.
The weights take fractional values between 0 and 1. If unspecified,
both weights default to 1, resulting in equal prioritization.
Interfacing with the application owner. Aceso exposes a terminal-
based configuration interface, with support for a dashboard offering
real-time monitoring, parameter updates, and notifications. Owners
provide inputs as key-value command-line arguments, enabling
reconfiguration without code or file modifications. Updated param-
eters can be submitted at any time and Aceso reloads and applies
them immediately, avoiding system restarts. For each new place-
ment decision, the owner receives a notification with associated
carbon and cost metrics. Only structural application changes (e.g.,
new services or altered dependencies) require system reinitializa-
tion. This interface offers flexible and transparent control over cost,
carbon, and performance trade-offs.
System Initialization. During initialization, Aceso prepares each
component for runtime operation. The Traffic Forecaster trains a
short-term prediction model using historical request traces, while
the Workload Profiler collects per-microservice performance met-
rics under representative workloads. This data collection and of-
fline training phase spans one week to capture recurring traffic
and usage patterns, which are predominant in cloud microservice
workloads [16, 18, 32, 48, 72, 85, 129]. The Placement Optimizer
performs an initial region filtering step to exclude suboptimal de-
ployment locations based on real-time carbon intensity and cost
data. Meanwhile, the Scheduler pre-replicates container images
across the filtered candidate regions to enable fast service migra-
tion. These steps are executed once before entering online mode
and are described in more detail in the following subsections.
4.2
Traffic Forecaster
The Traffic Forecaster predicts short-term user traffic to enable
proactive and adaptive microservice placement, since prediction
for short-term windows has been shown to be more accurate than
longer-term forecasting [27, 60, 80]. It continuously monitors ingress
traffic at the entry point (e.g., nginx or Envoy Gateway), capturing
traffic rates in real time. The forecaster operates in two phases,
Offline Training and Online Inference.
Offline Training. During system initialization, Aceso collects real
user traces (timestamps, traffic volumes) over several days. These
are used to train one lightweight Gradient Boosted Decision Tree
(GBDT) for short-term traffic prediction with a 1-hour forecasting
horizon. This granularity is chosen to match the update frequency
of regional carbon-intensity and pricing signals [2, 8]. GBDTs are
well suited for this setting because they handle tabular workload
traces, capture non-linear patterns with little training data, and offer
fast inference, making them a common choice in ML-for-systems
workloads [22, 70, 94, 114]. For each forecasting interval, predicted
values are aggregated, and the maximum is used as the load estimate
for the next hour. This conservative approach smooths transient
spikes and prevents oscillations, while remaining responsive to
meaningful workload changes. Since latency-sensitive applications
often exhibit bursts lasting only 15–45 minutes [118, 126], this
aggregation filters out short-lived fluctuations without sacrificing
adaptability. Section 5.4.2 presents further details to justify the final
model selection, among a variety of evaluated prediction models.
Online Inference. At runtime, the forecaster predicts short-term
traffic by running model inference every 1 hour. At timestamp 𝑡, it
outputs Traffic(𝑡+1), representing the expected load for the next
hour. Both the historical window and forecasting horizon use a 5-
minute granularity, so each prediction is based on the most recent
12 samples (covering the past hour), as also done in [45, 60, 61, 84,
91, 97].
Adaptivity. Aceso performs periodic retraining during low-load
periods (e.g., weekly at night) to maintain accuracy and adapt to
gradual workload shifts, a common practice in production sys-
tems [70, 85, 119].
4.3
Workload Profiler
The Workload Profiler characterizes how each microservice re-
sponds to varying traffic loads. It translates forecasted application-
level traffic into per-microservice metrics, such as latency, energy,
and resource usage, to help guide placement decisions.
Offline Profiling. During system initialization, the profiler sam-
ples the CPU, memory, network usage, internal latency, and en-
ergy consumption of each microservice under the observed work-
load traffic. These measurements populate a hash table indexed
by traffic buckets, which is shown to be an effective way for fast
lookups [39, 71]. Traffic measurements are processed at a 5-minute
granularity, using average values for all metrics.
To ensure tractable lookup and generalization, Aceso discretizes
the observed traffic range into 𝐾equal-frequency buckets. Let 𝑁
be the number of profiling samples and 𝑁min = 100 the minimum
samples per bucket. We set 𝐾= min(10, ⌊𝑁/𝑁min⌋), with a default
lower threshold of 𝐾min = 3. If any bucket contains fewer than 𝑁min
samples, adjacent buckets are merged until all meet the threshold.
This preserves equal-frequency semantics while ensuring statisti-
cal stability. For each bucket, the profiler computes a conservative
representative value for each metric. By default, this is the mean
plus one standard deviation (𝜇+ 𝜎), though upper percentiles (e.g.,
85th percentile) may be used for stronger tail conservatism. Each
bucket stores H [Traffic(𝑡), MS𝑖] = [𝐸𝑖(𝑡), 𝐿𝑖(𝑡),𝑆𝑖(𝑡)] for each mi-
croservice 𝑖and timestep 𝑡, where 𝐸is energy, 𝐿is internal latency,
and 𝑆= [CPU, Mem, Net] is the resource footprint.
Online Lookup. At runtime, the profiler receives the forecasted
traffic from the Traffic Forecaster and performs a millisecond-scale
lookup in the hash table H (𝑡+ 1) to retrieve, for each microservice,
the matching bucket 𝑀𝑆𝑖and corresponding metrics, enabling the
optimizer to evaluate placement feasibility, carbon impact, and cost.
Adaptivity. The Workload Profiler continuously refreshes its hash
table in the background, maintaining up-to-date metrics for each
microservice over the past week and discarding older values to
ensure relevance. When the table grows beyond 2000 samples, the
profiler selectively forgets the oldest entries, as also done in [56].
This background refresh is designed to operate independently of
retraining or software updates.
6


--- Page 7 ---
4.4
Placement Optimizer
The Placement Optimizer assigns movable microservices to geo-
distributed regions to minimize carbon emissions and cost while
satisfying latency SLOs. It solves a constrained single-objective
optimization problem that minimizes a weighted sum of carbon
and cost, subject to latency and dependency constraints, over a
discrete set of microservices and candidate regions.
Modeling thousands of microservices across multiple regions
with latency-aware dependencies creates an enormous search space
that must be navigated efficiently at runtime. For this reason, Aceso
uses a genetic algorithm (GA) [54, 63], which offers a scalable al-
ternative by evolving a population of candidate placements over
time. Each solution encodes a mapping of movable microservices to
regions. The algorithm iteratively selects high-quality placements
(those with low carbon, low cost and latency that satisfies the SLO),
recombines them via crossover, and introduces diversity through
mutation. Infeasible solutions are discarded, and the process contin-
ues until convergence, or a time budget is reached. This approach
allows Aceso to find near-optimal placements in a scalable way.
Search space pruning. Aceso introduces microservice pinning and
region filtering as key optimizations to reduce search space and
preserve latency guarantees.
1. Microservice Pinning reduces the optimization search space
by excluding microservices that must remain in their base region.
First, Aceso structurally pins microservices that are inherently im-
movable due to operational or regulatory constraints, such as fron-
tends that must remain close to users for responsiveness, and
databases that are bound by data sovereignty laws [25, 50, 65]. Sec-
ond, Aceso leverages our insight from Section 3, that moving later-
activated microservices and disrupting the critical path introduces
severe latency delays. Therefore, Aceso ranks microservices based
on their activation order and pins the ones that are later activated,
marking a smaller percentage as “movable”. Aceso starts pinning
late-activated microservices when the end-to-end latency observed
when all microservices execute in their base region approaches the
SLO, for example when it reaches about 80–90% of the SLO [28].
Together, these mechanisms ensure that only latency-tolerant and
legally movable microservices are considered for geo-distributed
placement, effectively reducing the search space.
2. Region Filtering further reduces the search space by pruning
suboptimal regions using real-time carbon intensity and pricing
data. Region pruning follows a Pareto-style rule, which retains
regions only if they do not worsen all metrics compared to the
base region. To prevent cost increases, something that is critical
for SMEs, Aceso applies a conservative dominance rule, when both
carbon and cost are active objectives. More specifically, a region
that improves only one metric is discarded if another region strictly
improves both carbon and cost over the base region. Filtering occurs
during initialization and before each optimization cycle, enabling
Aceso to adapt to evolving cost–carbon conditions.
Optimization Execution. For each candidate placement, that is
the (microservice, region) combinations of the pruned search space,
the optimizer calculates the following three metrics using inputs
from the profiler, the application owner, and external APIs. First, it
computes end-to-end latency by combining internal microservice
latencies provided by the Workload Profiler with inter- and intra-
region RTTs retrieved from external APIs [7]. Second, it computes
total carbon emissions by using per-microservice energy usage
from the Workload Profiler together with regional carbon intensity
obtained from real-time carbon APIs. Those values are multiplied
to derive the carbon footprint of each microservice, as proposed
in related works [56, 68]. Third, it computes deployment mone-
tary cost by mapping resource demands supplied by the Workload
Profiler to the smallest compatible cloud instance type, using live
pricing information from the corresponding API. Application-level
inputs, such as the application structure, latency SLOs, and allowed
regions, are provided by the application owner and guide the fea-
sibility checks during the optimization. The optimization result is
a mapping of each movable microservice to its assigned region
(microservice, region). In summary, the optimizer solves:
min
assignment 𝑤carbon
∑︁
𝑚∈M
CI𝑟(𝑚) · 𝐸𝑚+ 𝑤cost
∑︁
𝑚∈M
𝑃𝑟(𝑚),𝑠𝑚
s.t. Latency(assignment) ≤SLO
where M is the set of microservices, 𝑟(𝑚) the assigned region,
CI𝑟(𝑚) its carbon intensity, 𝐸𝑚the energy of 𝑚, 𝑃𝑟(𝑚),𝑠𝑚the price
for size 𝑠𝑚, and 𝑤carbon,𝑤cost are user-defined weights. The GA-
based optimizer has time complexity O(𝑀2) and memory com-
plexity O(𝑅2 + 𝑀2), where 𝑀= |M| is the number of movable
microservices and 𝑅= |R| is the number of candidate regions. Our
insight-driven pruning reduces these to O(𝑀′2) and O(𝑅′2 + 𝑀′2)
with 𝑀′ < 𝑀and 𝑅′ < 𝑅, leaving the asymptotic Big-O unchanged
while lowering the constants. For example, if microservice pinning
reduces 𝑀by one-third (𝑀′ = 2𝑀
3 ) and region filtering halves 𝑅
(𝑅′ = 𝑅
2 ), runtime becomes ( 2
3)2 = 4
9 of the original (approximately
44.4%). Memory becomes 1
4𝑅2 + 4
9𝑀2, i.e., between 1
4 and 4
9 of the
original depending on whether region or microservice terms domi-
nate.
Event-driven Optimization. While the Traffic Forecaster and
Workload Profiler run periodically, the Placement Optimizer is
event-driven and re-invoked when triggered by either carbon or
workload changes.
Carbon-driven trigger: Aceso monitors carbon intensity forecasts
via CarbonCast [75] and triggers optimization when a region’s
predicted carbon intensity deviates significantly (more than 20%)
from its recent trend [23, 74, 119]. For example this happens due to
a transition from day to night and vice versa (Figure 1).
Workload-driven trigger: The optimizer reacts to forecasted traffic
changes. When predicted load shifts to a new bucket, indicating
a significant deviation from current capacity, it triggers reopti-
mization. For example, if the system is deployed for medium traffic
(100–500 requests/sec) at 300 requests/sec, and forecasts an increase
to high traffic (500-700 requests/sec) with 600 requests/sec, this shift
triggers reoptimization to adjust the deployment for the anticipated
higher load.
4.5
Scheduler
The Scheduler enacts the placement plan produced by the Opti-
mizer. For each (microservice, region) assignment, the scheduler
provisions resources in the target region and instantiates the cor-
responding microservices from pre-replicated images. The images
7


--- Page 8 ---
were pre-replicated during Aceso’s initialization to enable fast tran-
sitions in geo-distributed placement at minimal cost (e.g., AWS
ECR storage costs $0.10/GB per month [5]). The scheduler next
verifies microservice readiness through control-plane health and
dependency checks before rerouting traffic to the new instances, en-
suring SLO compliance. Once stable, the scheduler decommissions
old instances and performs resource cleanup.
4.6
Implementation Details
Aceso is deployed on Kubernetes and integrates cloud-native tools
and APIs across its components:
• Traffic Forecaster: Runs as a sidecar container next to the
ingress gateway (NGINX or Envoy). It uses Prometheus [13] to
collect traffic metrics and runs a lightweight ML model deployed
with scikit-learn [83] and NeuralForecast [53], a PyTorch-based
library for advanced time series forecasting.
• Workload Profiler: Runs as a centralized database pod that
stores microservice-specific telemetry. It samples CPU, memory,
network, latency, and energy metrics via Prometheus, using
Node Exporter for resource metrics and Kepler for energy data,
and organizes them in a hash table indexed by traffic buckets.
• Placement Optimizer: Runs as a control-plane pod execut-
ing a custom Python-based Genetic Algorithm. It queries the
Electricity Maps API [9] for carbon intensity, the AWS Pricing
Calculator [8] for compute costs, and the AWS Latency Monitor-
ing API [7] for inter-region RTTs.
• Scheduler: Runs as a control-plane pod. It uses Kubernetes for
pod scheduling, Liqo [12] for multi-region orchestration, and
the Vertical Pod Autoscaler [11] for autoscaling. Microservices
are deployed via Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) [6]
on Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) instances, with container im-
ages distributed through Amazon Elastic Container Registry
(ECR) [5].
5
EVALUATION
We evaluate Aceso to demonstrate its ability to meet the three sys-
tem objectives introduced in Section 4: (O1) carbon efficiency, (O2)
cost efficiency, and (O3) latency compliance under strict SLOs. Our
evaluation spans four dimensions: (i) a real-world deployment using
the DeathStarBench social network application [1, 51] to validate
the viability, practicality and benefits of Aceso (Section 5.1); (ii)
a comparison against state-of-the-art baselines, including heuris-
tic and exact optimization approaches, to quantify trade-offs in
carbon footprint, cost, latency, and optimization overhead (Sec-
tion 5.2); (iii) scalability analysis across increasing microservice
application sizes, candidate regions and SLO latency requirements
(Section 5.3); and (iv) an ablation study isolating the impact of key
design components such as microservice pinning, region filtering,
and traffic forecasting (Section 5.4). Together, these experiments
provide a comprehensive assessment of Aceso ’s effectiveness in
balancing sustainability, performance and practicality for SMEs
with microservice applications.
5.1
Real-world Deployment
We deploy the DeathStarBench [51] social network application,
following the same methodology as in Section 3. The experiment

Figure 7: Microservice placement (geo-distribution) across
regions over 4 days. Vertical dashed lines mark optimization
triggers.
spans four consecutive days to evaluate Aceso under realistic oper-
ational variability. Incoming requests are generated according to a
Poisson process, with arrival rates scaled based on transition pat-
terns observed in Azure Functions production traces [127], a trace
commonly used to evaluate related works [56, 62, 79, 88, 100, 124].
In this experiment, we consider two distinct base locations: an EU-
based SME in Frankfurt and a North America-based SME in Calgary,
using AWS for deployment. These regions represent contrasting
carbon profiles and regulatory contexts: Frankfurt (eu-central-1)
has moderate carbon intensity typical of European grids, while
Calgary (ca-west-1) reflects Alberta’s higher-carbon electricity mix.
Candidate regions are restricted to EU and North America locations,
respectively, to ensure compliance with data sovereignty regula-
tions. The SLO is set to 300 ms, which is a typical value used for
the social network application of DeathStarBench [28, 71].
Figure 6 presents the normalized carbon emissions and cost
over a 96-hour period for Aceso compared to the static baseline
that keeps all microservices in Frankfurt and Calgary, respectively.
Across time, Aceso achieves an average carbon reduction of 37.4%
and cost savings of 3.6%, which includes compute, storage, and
transfer overheads, for the EU-based scenario. In the North America-
based scenario, Aceso achieves an average reduction in carbon
emissions of 21.2%. In this case the cost doesn’t change, since the
AWS resources in Calgary and Montreal have the same cost [8]. The
8


--- Page 9 ---
oscillations in carbon and cost correspond to the migration patterns
in Figure 7, which depicts the distribution of microservices across
regions and marks with vertical dashed lines the times where the
Placement Optimizer was triggered due to load fluctuations.
During predicted load spikes (Figure 7), most services return to
Frankfurt and Calgary to preserve latency compliance, temporarily
increasing carbon emissions and cost. When load stabilizes, Aceso
resumes offloading to greener regions such as Stockholm, Paris and
Montreal, restoring carbon and cost benefits. On average, Aceso
triggers 6–7 placement changes per day, relocating approximately
42% of microservices per change in the European setting and 12%
in the North-America setting. Thanks to region filtering and mi-
croservice pinning, these placements remain well below the SLO
threshold. In Europe, Aceso achieves an average end-to-end latency
of 93.8ms ms and only 0.2% violations, and in North America, Aceso
achieves an average end-to-end latency of 40.1 ms and only 0.1%
violations.
Takeaways: This interplay between Figures 6 and 7 demonstrates
Aceso ’s ability to dynamically balance carbon and cost efficiency
with strict SLO adherence under real-world deployments, by dy-
namically placing microservices across selected regions. In this
way, Aceso successfully realizes all the system objectives described
in Section 4, delivering carbon and cost efficiency under latency
compliance.
5.2
Comparison Against Baselines
We compare Aceso against a diverse set of baselines that represent
common strategies for microservice placement, spanning heuristic
and exact optimization approaches:
• Nautilus[48]: dynamically reallocates microservices across nodes
within the same geographical region to preserve QoS. For our
purposes, this serves as a single region static baseline without
geo-distributed placement.
• Caribou[56]: applies heuristic-biased stochastic sampling for
carbon-aware spatial shifting of serverless workloads. It con-
siders carbon and cost but lacks fine-grained latency control
required for microservices.
• Linear Programming (LP): We include LP as an oracle base-
line to approximate optimal placement under carbon, cost, and
latency constraints. The problem is inherently non-linear, as de-
scribed in Section 3, making exact LP formulations intractable at
scale. Following standard relaxation techniques [17, 46, 121], we
construct a linearized LP approximation that captures dominant
trade-offs while remaining computationally feasible.
• Vanilla Genetic Algorithm (GA): A baseline inspired by prior
GA-based optimizers [35], applying standard evolutionary search
over the full placement space without any pruning or latency-
aware constraints.
• Aceso: Our proposed GA-based optimizer that incorporates insight-
driven pruning, including region filtering and microservice pin-
ning, to allow for scalability and latency compliance.
Real-world experimental validation. We revisit the motivational
experiment introduced in Section 3, where a social network appli-
cation from the DeathStarBench [1, 51] was deployed across Spain
(base region) and Sweden. For the initial static placement, the bot-
tom of figure 3b shows that Aceso converges to the same static place-
ment as the LP-based oracle (OPT) and vanilla GA, confirming its
ability to find near-optimal solutions under real-world constraints.
Caribou produces a similar configuration, excluding microservice
M7. Figure 4 illustrates that Aceso reduces carbon emissions to
0.79× the Spain baseline (21% reduction), lowers monthly cost to
0.982× (about 1.8% cheaper), and maintains latency at 111.13 ms
with only 1.67% SLO violations, which matches the optimal trade-
off. To extend this validation beyond static placements, we next
rely on larger-scale, simulation-based experiments for dynamic
scenarios.
Simulation-based experiments. We developed a high-fidelity
simulation environment that models multi-region cloud infrastruc-
tures and large-scale microservice applications. The simulator incor-
porates real-world latency measurements [7] across AWS regions,
region-specific carbon intensity [2] and pricing data [8], and empir-
ical execution profiles for typical microservice types. It propagates
requests through complete microservice DAGs under varying traffic
loads, computing end-to-end latency, carbon emissions, and opera-
tional cost for each placement. This approach enables controlled
experiments with hundreds to thousands of microservices and dy-
namic workload changes. We validate the simulator’s fidelity by
reproducing the real-world experiment from Section 3, observing
an average error of less than 2% for latency, 3% for carbon, and 0.5%
for cost.
Experimental setup. We simulate an EU-based microservice ap-
plication with 100 services, reflecting the typical deployment size
of SMEs as discussed in Section 2.1. The simulation uses the same
four-day workload as the real-world experiment. Frankfurt serves
as the base region, representing an AWS location with medium car-
bon intensity (Figure 1) and medium–high cost (Figure 2). Only EU
regions are considered to comply with data sovereignty constraints.
To allow all baselines to converge, we relax strict latency SLOs in
this experiment. Next, we present detailed results comparing Aceso
against these baselines.
Carbon Efficiency. Figure 8a shows the normalized average carbon
emissions over four days, relative to Nautilus, which keeps all mi-
croservices in Frankfurt. Aceso achieves the best practical carbon
savings, reducing emissions to 0.48× Nautilus, slightly outperform-
ing Caribou (0.49×) and significantly better than vanilla GA (0.63×).
LP obtains the lowest emissions overall (0.37×) but is not scalable,
highlighting that Aceso delivers near-optimal carbon efficiency.
Cost savings. Figure 8b reports normalized average cost relative
to Nautilus, accounting for compute resources, storage, and data
transfer. All methods exhibit similar cost profiles, with differences
under 4%. Aceso incurs 0.984×Nautilus, slightly higher than Caribou
(0.971×) and LP (0.959×), but significantly better than vanilla GA
(0.996×). These results confirm that Aceso, is able to reduce both
carbon emissions and cost, enabling non-negligible cost savings.
End-to-end latency. Figure 8c compares the average end-to-end la-
tency across methods. Aceso achieves 1.33 s, outperforming Caribou
(1.55 s) and GA (1.61 s), while LP obtains slightly lower latency at
1.28 s. Nautilus shows the lowest latency (0.34 s) as it does not per-
form cross-region migrations. These results indicate that Aceso
9

(b) Responsiveness and stability metrics.
Table 1: Microservice placement across regions and baselines.
dynamically places the 100 microservices across regions in a way
that allows for latency to be comparable to the optimal placement.
Solve time. Figure 8d reports the average optimization time for
each method. Aceso converges in 25.8 s, significantly faster than
Caribou (12.3 min) and GA (1.4 min), while LP is impractical at 4.41 h.
Nautilus incurs zero solve time as it does not perform optimization.
These results demonstrate that Aceso achieves effective placements
with very low overhead, enabling responsiveness under dynamic
conditions.
Geo-distribution. Table 1a shows the the average number of mi-
croservices placed in each region by the different baselines. Nautilus
keeps all services in Frankfurt (100%), acting as a static baseline. LP
spreads aggressively to minimize carbon and cost, sending most
services to Stockholm (65.4%) and Paris (9.6%), both low-carbon and
low-cost regions (Figures 1, 2). Caribou and GA result in spreading
microservices across most of the European countries. In contrast,
Aceso prunes inefficient regions like Ireland and Milan, prioritizing
greener options such as Paris (39.9%) and Stockholm (23.9%) for
balanced placement that reduces both emissions and cost.
10 1001000
Number of Microservices
1
10
100
AVG Solve Time (s)
10 s
0.4 s
2.2 min
EU
EU+US
All
(a) Solve time vs. microservice
count and region set.
10 1001000
Number of Microservices
1
10

AVG Solve Time (s)
8.5 s
0.4 s
2.3 min
Relaxed
Medium
Strict
(b) Solve time vs. microservice
count and SLO strictness.
Figure 9: Scalability of Aceso.
Responsiveness and stability. Table 1b summarizes responsiveness
and stability across baselines. In one day, optimization is triggered
41 times due to workload or carbon changes. Adaptation Rate is the
percentage of triggers that lead to a new geo-distribution. Aceso,
GA and LP react to 100% of triggers, far higher than Caribou (16.6%)
that fails to respond, due to more conservative placement decisions
during low load. MS moved shows the fraction of microservices
moved, on average, across all new geo-distributions. Caribou moves
100%, relocating all services each time, being more prone to system
instability. LP moves only 3%, reflecting minimal changes after
its aggressive initial distribution. Aceso strikes a balance, moving
52,2% microservices, on average, reducing changes in placements
from GA (68.3%). This demonstrates that Aceso avoids excessive
migrations while maintaining responsiveness to workloads and
carbon changes.
Takeaways: Across all metrics, Aceso offers the most practical bal-
ance of sustainability, performance, and stability, compared to other
baselines. It achieves near-optimal carbon reduction and competi-
tive cost savings while maintaining latency close to the optimal LP
solution. Most importantly, Aceso leverages sophisticated search
space pruning to converge in seconds rather than minutes or hours,
while prioritizing regions that maximize carbon and cost benefits.
This enables real-time responsiveness under dynamic conditions,
making Aceso the only solution that combines efficiency, stability,
and adaptability
5.3
Scalability
Building on the comparison in Section 5.2, where Aceso was the
only solution to converge within seconds, we now evaluate its
scalability as application size and candidate region count increase.
Figure 9a shows the Placement Optimizer’s solve time as we increase
the number of microservices (x-axis) and candidate regions (EU,
EU+US, All). Even with 1,000 microservices and all global regions,
Aceso converges in under 2.2 minutes, and takes seconds for small
deployments. This scalability is enabled by insight-driven pruning,
which significantly reduces the search space.
Next, we evaluate Aceso’s ability to scale as the optimization
problem becomes harder to solve, which occurs under stricter SLO
latencies. Figure 9b reports solve time under three SLO scales: re-
laxed (no effective SLO), medium and strict (latency reaches 80%
and 90% of the SLO, following [28]). With 100 microservices, solve
time decreases under stricter SLOs because Aceso prunes more in-
feasible placements. With 1,000 microservices, solve time is largely
10


--- Page 11 ---
10
100
1000
Microservice Size
101
102
103
104
Search Space
8
80
800
13
128
1280
40
400
4000
64
640
6400
Aceso
W/o Region pruning
W/o MS pruning
W/o both
Figure 10: Ablation study of the Placement Optimizer.
unaffected, as complexity is dominated by the number of candidate
region-microservice mappings even after pruning.
These results show that Aceso handles SME-scale deployments
(Section 2.1) in a few seconds, far faster than other approaches,
as shown in Figure 8d. Because workload bursts last 15–45 min-
utes [118, 126] and carbon shifts occur at coarse diurnal intervals [2],
optimization is triggered only every few hours (Figure 11). Aceso
consistently solves placements well within these intervals, main-
taining responsiveness and adaptability. Our experiments confirm
that none of the other baselines scale beyond 100 microservices
or strict SLOs, which is also stated in other prior works [95, 123],
making Aceso the only approach capable of operating at realistic
deployment scales.
5.4
Ablation Study
5.4.1
Optimizer. To understand the impact of our search space
pruning strategies, we perform an ablation study on the Placement
Optimizer. We compare Aceso against three variants: without re-
gion filtering, without microservice pinning, and without both. This
isolates the contribution of each technique to scalability. Figure 10
shows the size of the search space in logarithmic scale under these
configurations. Aceso achieves the smallest search space thanks
to combined pruning, compared to the unoptimized variant (w/o
both). Removing either region filtering or microservice pinning
significantly increases the search space, while disabling both leads
to almost 1 order of magnitude larger search space. These results con-
firm that insight-driven pruning is critical for scalability, enabling
Aceso to converge in seconds up to minutes for larger deployments,
as seen in Section 5.3.
5.4.2
Forecaster. To evaluate the impact of the Traffic Forecaster,
we repeat our baseline experiment (Section 5.2) while disabling
the predictor. In this reactive mode, any load increase or decrease
detected by the Workload Profiler immediately triggers placement
changes. Our analysis shows that reacting to workload changes
leads to trivial differences in carbon emissions, cost, and latency;
each within ±1% of the original values. However, the absence of
forecasting substantially decreases system stability. The number
of optimization triggers rises from 27 to 113, and Aceso reacts to
71.6% of these triggers (compared to 100% with forecasting). This
results in a 4× increase in change of placements, from an average
of 6-7 changes per day with the forecaster to 28-29 without it.
Evaluation of the Traffic Forecaster. We evaluated traffic predic-
tion using the Microsoft Azure Functions Invocation Trace [127], a
dataset widely used in prior work for training and evaluating similar
models [56, 62, 79, 88, 100, 124]. The dataset, sampled at five-minute
intervals, was split into 50% training, 21% validation, and 29% testing
00:00
04:00
08:00
12:00
16:00
20:00
24:00
Time (Hours)
0
0.25
0.5
0.75
1
Requests
Ground Truth
GBDT (MAE=0.039)
RandomForest (MAE=0.041)
LinearRegression (MAE=0.041)
Prophet (MAE=0.049)
NHITS (MAE=0.056)
PatchTST (MAE=0.065)
Figure 11: Traffic prediction over 24 hours using different
models; optimization triggers are marked with vertical lines.
corresponding to 7, 3, and 4 days worth of data respectively. We eval-
uate a representative spectrum of forecasting approaches: simple
linear models (Linear Regression), ensemble tree learners (Random
Forest [24], Gradient Boosted Decision Trees-GBDT [47]), a widely
used and robust statistical model (Prophet [36, 106]), and recent neu-
ral forecasting architectures (N-HiTS [27], PatchTST [80]). Several
systems papers have employed the above models to predict re-
source usage [22, 94, 122, 125], guide scheduling decisions [70, 114],
and support performance management [49, 59, 64], motivating the
choice behind the model selection.
Figure 11 illustrates predictions over 48 hours. GBDT achieved
the lowest Mean Absolute Error with sub-millisecond inference
time, outperforming all baselines. Random Forest and Linear Re-
gression followed closely but tended to underpredict traffic spikes.
Prophet captured seasonal trends but failed to model short-term fluc-
tuations leading to noticeable deviations during peak traffic hours.
Deep learning models N-HiTS and PatchTST underperformed for
similar reasons: both architectures are designed for long-horizon
forecasting and require large historical windows, which were un-
available in the dataset. Maintaining such long histories would also
increase resource overhead and delay system initialization.
The results above reinforce observations from related works
that demonstrate how lightweight predictors often suffice for short-
horizon traffic forecasting, making complex deep learning models
unnecessary in such settings [20, 30–33, 38, 82, 85, 86]. In conclu-
sion, Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT) is the most accurate
and efficient choice for real-time forecasting in Aceso.
6
RELATED WORK
Interest in carbon-aware solutions for the IT sector is growing,
driven by the increasing energy demand of cloud and edge infras-
tructures [120]. Prior studies have explored how to reduce carbon
emissions, costs, and latency, though typically addressing these ob-
jectives separately. In contrast, this work aims to jointly optimize all
three (reduce carbon and cost, while preserving SLO latency com-
pliance) in the context of microservice-based applications, which
dominate modern distributed systems. Table 2 summarizes the most
relevant studies and highlights how our approach differs from ex-
isting work.
Several efforts have proposed carbon-aware autoscaling. Car-
bonScaler [57] dynamically adjusts the number of servers for batch
workloads based on carbon intensity forecasts and workload elas-
ticity, reducing emissions while maintaining performance. Car-
bonEdge [117] focuses on spatial shifting, relocating applications
11


--- Page 12 ---
Solutions
MS
App
Latency
Compl.
Carbon
Aware
Cost
Efficient
CarbonScaler [57],
Let’s Wait a While [115]
✗
✗
✓
✗
CarbonEdge [117], Ecolife [62],
KEIDS [67], S.C.A.L.E[37]
✗
✓
✓
✗
GAIA [58]
✗
✗
✓
✓
Oakestra [19], Astraea [125],
Nautilus [48], FIRM [87],
Imbres [71]
✓
✓
✗
✗
Caribou [56]
✗
✓
✓
✓
NSGA-II Opt [35]
✓
✓
✗
✓
CASPER [102], PCAPS[69]
✓
✓
✓
✗
Aceso
✓
✓
✓
✓
Table 2: Summary of related deployment or orchestration
frameworks and their key attributes, microservice applica-
tion (MS App), latency compliance, carbon awareness and
cost efficiency, compared to Aceso.
to regions with lower carbon intensity to exploit geographical
variations. GAIA [58] works for flexible, compute-intensive batch
workloads, that are not latency critical, and performs only temporal
shifting within a single geographic region. Caribou [56] focuses
on serverless applications. Both GAIA and Caribou incorporate
cost-awareness, arguing that organizations are more likely to adopt
carbon-aware strategies when these also lower, or at least do not
increase,operational costs. Our work follows a similar rationale
aiming to reduce operational costs.
In the serverless domain, Ecolife [62] aligns function execu-
tion with periods of lower carbon intensity and leverages multi-
generation hardware. Works considering microservices, such as
Nautilus [48] and Oakestra [19], provide orchestration across dis-
tributed environments but do not account for carbon or monetary
cost. In our study, we use Kubernetes as an orchestration frame-
work within each region, though Oakestra could also serve as an
alternative for edge deployments [19]. Furthermore, load-balancing
frameworks such as Nautilus [48], FIRM [87] and Imbres [71] are
complementary to our work: while they optimize resource usage
and performance within a region, Aceso operates across regions
to minimize carbon footprint and cost under latency constraints.
Let’s Wait a While [115] explores temporal shifting, scheduling
workloads such as ML training or maintenance tasks during cleaner
energy periods. However, as power grids decarbonize, the benefits
of temporal shifting diminish [103]. Moreover, for latency-critical
applications, which is the focus of this paper, such deferrals are
impractical.
Complementary work includes holistic frameworks like Carbon-
Explorer [14], which supports carbon-aware datacenter design, and
GreenSKUs [112], which proposes low-carbon server configura-
tions. Aceso targets the real-world majority (small and medium-
sized enterprises, SMEs) and operates on widely deployed cloud
infrastructures. Finally, the Sunk Carbon Fallacy [21] challenges
traditional carbon accounting by emphasizing the negative impact
of embodied carbon in scheduling decisions and proposing metrics
that better capture the true environmental impact of computing.
7
DISCUSSION & FUTURE WORK
Current limitations of Aceso. While Aceso demonstrates signif-
icant carbon and cost savings under latency constraints, several
limitations remain. First, the optimizer depends on real-time carbon-
intensity and pricing signals, which may occasionally be stale or
delayed, affecting decision accuracy despite our local caching mech-
anisms. Second, our evaluation focuses on AWS-based deployments;
generalizing to other cloud providers, or hybrid infrastructures
may introduce new challenges, such as different microservice inter-
communication latencies, data transfer policies, or region-specific
regulatory constraints. Finally, Aceso assumes containerized mi-
croservices and Kubernetes orchestration, limiting applicability to
legacy or monolithic applications.
Future Work. Future research will extend Aceso along multiple
directions. We plan to: 1) explore integration with carbon credit
markets [26, 44, 55] and spot pricing to further reduce operational
costs; 2) enhance the system with tiered SLOs, to enable differen-
tiated service levels for premium and best-effort users and tasks;
3) develop a dashboard for real-time monitoring and interactive
configuration, to improve usability and practicality for SMEs.
8
SUMMARY
This paper presents Aceso, a system that dynamically places mi-
croservices across regions by optimizing for carbon emissions and
cost, while meeting latency requirements, using a scalable opti-
mization algorithm that leverages an insight-based pruned search
space.Unlike prior work that focused on batch or serverless work-
loads, Aceso supports latency-sensitive microservices and operates
effectively and cost-efficiently even under regional deployment con-
straints. Our extensive evaluation demonstrates that Aceso achieves
significant carbon and cost savings while preserving application
performance, offering a practical path towards sustainable microser-
vice deployment for the real-world majority: small to medium-sized
enterprises (SMEs).

on Cloud Computing (San Francisco, California) (SoCC ’22). Association for
Computing Machinery, New York, NY, USA, 210–225. https://doi.org/10.1145/

vices. In 2024 IEEE International Symposium on High-Performance Computer
